name: Praseodymium
name_origin: From Greek 'prasios' (leek-green) and 'didymos' (twin), referring to the green colour of its salts and its origin from didymium.

occurrence.abundance_summary: Moderately abundant rare-earth (~9.2 ppm in crust); always found together with other lanthanides, chiefly in monazite and bastnaesite ores.

compounds.name: Praseodymium(III) oxide
compounds.summary: Pale green ceramic colourant used in glasses and enamels; forms part of the cubic 'Pr6O11' mixed-valence oxide.

compounds.name: Praseodymium oxide (mixed valence)
compounds.summary: Black powder used as a yellow pigment in ceramics ('praseodymium yellow') and as an oxidation catalyst.

compounds.name: Praseodymium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Green crystalline salt used as a precursor to other praseodymium compounds and in research catalysis.

compounds.name: Praseodymium(III) fluoride
compounds.summary: Used in carbon-arc lamps and as a starting material for praseodymium-based phosphors and lasers.

uses: Didymium glass for welder's and glassblower's safety goggles (filters yellow light)
Yellow ceramic and glass pigment ('praseodymium yellow')
Component in high-strength NdFeB permanent magnets
Praseodymium-doped fluoride glass (PDFA) optical signal amplifiers
Misch metal alloys in lighter flints
Studio carbon-arc lamps and aircraft engine alloys
reactivity_summary: Soft, silvery-yellow metal; develops a green oxide coating in air that spalls off, exposing more metal. Reacts slowly with cold water and rapidly with acids. Burns readily in oxygen.
isotopes_summary: Praseodymium has only one stable, naturally occurring isotope (Pr-141). Many synthetic radioisotopes are known; Pr-143 (half-life 13.6 days) is a notable fission product.

citations.source: Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Praseodymium
